---
description: "A note on process. This is live, in-progress work written in the open, with AI in the loop and the judgment mine."
toc: false
---

# How This Book Is Being Written {.unnumbered}

A note on process, since you are here and it matters. This book is written in the
open, which means it is a live experiment as much as it is a text. It is my
sabbatical project, and from July 2026 through the first half of 2027 my main work
is to build it out.

The question I am chasing is how AI can genuinely help us do computer
architecture. It is easy to have opinions about AI from the sidelines. I think it
is harder, and more useful, to work with these systems every day and feel where
they help and where they fail. If I am going to argue that engineering is changing
because of AI, I should experience that change firsthand rather than theorize
about it from a distance. And this is not only about writing. Alongside the book I
am building code that I hope to release with it. All of it is skunkworks, out in
the open.

Let me be candid about how I work. I am leaning hard on generative AI, not to write
faster but as a bet on the new capacities these systems can unlock for architecture
work itself. I experiment with agentic workflows and other creative uses of these
tools to find where that bet pays off and where it does not. Pushing this hard means
some of what you find here will read rough, even sloppy, especially in the early
passes. Give it time. The work that matters is catching those places and making them
better, and that is most of what I actually do. The ideas, arguments, structure, and
direction are mine. I spend my time thinking through the questions I want to answer,
shaping the narrative, and deciding what the contribution is, and I then use a
workflow of several AI agents to brainstorm alternatives, challenge my assumptions,
reorganize material, and critique drafts. What I am still learning is how to govern
that process, knowing when to accept a suggestion, when to reject it outright, and
when fluent language is quietly hiding a weak idea. That judgment is mine to develop,
and I will not develop it by avoiding these tools.

I was at this part time for more than six months before opening it up, and the work
does not move in a straight line. It goes two steps forward and one step back,
sometimes more. When things seem to be going well, I get confident and let the
engine run, and that is often exactly when it inverts and I have to walk it back.

This is also new ground. I have been circling these ideas for about three years,
since an early IEEE article that set out the foundations, and there is no finished
resource to go read. So I am building the area with my research group, writing it in
pieces and trying to sift a coherent picture out of them.

There is a chicken-and-egg problem here. It is far easier to write once things have
settled, and this has not settled. Waiting for that would be the safe choice, but
the subject is taking shape right now, and I would rather help shape it than
document it after the fact. What makes writing possible this early is that we are
not starting from nothing. Adjacent fields such as safety engineering, electronic
design automation, experimental science, and machine learning have already worked
through versions of these problems, and there is real
room to innovate by borrowing what they learned and grounding it in the domain
expertise of computer architecture. Deciding what transfers, what does not, and
what we have to invent ourselves is where much of the contribution lives.

The surest way I know to understand something is to teach it, and I have never been
able to separate that from writing it down. It is the same advice I give my
students. Do not wait until the experiments are finished and the results are in to
start writing. Write while you are still working the problem, because putting it
into words is what shows you whether you actually understand it, and where you do
not. That is what I am doing here. This book is my attempt to teach a subject I am
still learning, and to understand it better by writing my way through it.

All of that to say, this is a living document, and I am editing it regularly. What
you read today may well read differently tomorrow, the day after, the next week.
Take what you see with a grain of salt until I sign off on it, which is still a few
months out. So why put it out now, rough as it is? Because I would rather paint with
the studio door open. People who work in public open themselves to criticism,
and they tend to get sharper because of it, not despite it. I have always believed
in building this way, and I do not see publishing as the end of the process. I see
it as the start of a conversation. If a passage is vague, imprecise, or wrong, or
if you just want to talk it through, I would genuinely like to hear it. Feel free to
[reach out](https://vijay.seas.harvard.edu).

Vijay Janapa Reddi
